rect.style: fill: light-dark(#ffffff, var(--ge-dark-color, #121212)); stroke: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(255, 255, 255));

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 408px; height: 1px; padding-top: 825px; margin-left: 1px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
What's happening?
When load the Netflix app on your smart TV, that TV sends a request containing the correct access token for your account to the router. It is the router's job to send that request 'packet' to the Internet Service provider (ISP).
The router sends ALL the requests from your local network to your ISP, using the public IP address as the sender address. Once it hits the server it requested (e.g., Netflix.com).
The server's job is to send back the account-specific data needed to load your watch history, titles, lists on your Netflix Home screen. It addresses the packet(s) TO the public IP address and sends the data through its own ISP out over the Internet back to your router.
Finally, your router, serving as the broker, keeps a record of which local client (phone, laptop, printer, etc.) made each request so it knows in this case to deliver the Netflix data back to 192.168.1.4, because that is the local IP address it assigned to the TV when the TV connected to the Wi-Fi.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 308px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1320px; margin-left: 1241px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #ffffff;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#ffffff, #121212); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.span: These special servers are the authoritative address book of the Internet. They keep track of the true address(es) that every domain (e.g., google.com) lives at.

A small domain, like my personal website, has a single IP address, whereas Google, Amazon, large services that serve millions of requests/second have MANY IP addresses! Google has 100s of thousands of IP addresses.

div.font: Google likely has 1000s of IP addresses just for the Southeast US alone, largely from massive data centers in Atlanta, GA.

div.font: The ISP's DNS resolver chooses IP address near you and forwards
your request to the server that lives there.
Without DNS, you'd have to memorize 142.250.80.46

div.div: Local IP addresses: Only unique to your home network. Not known by outside networks. (Not sensitive). Your router's way of knowing exactly "who" each device is.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 208px; height: 1px; padding-top: 436px; margin-left: 790px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #ffffff;

div.div:
Public IP addresses: These are the addresses everyone on the Internet can see. They are issued by the ISP.
Your router/modem use the public IP address as the FROM address, telling the servers they request data from (or upload data to) to respond back by sending the data TO that address.
This allows all the responses to make it back to the router, so it can forward along to the phone, laptop, smart camera, etc.
